test_id_1.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says dual-eligible pharmacy claims are limited by Medicare coordination rules. Medicare payment is accepted as payment in full, and if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, Medicaid makes no additional payment.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update does not state a direct dual-eligible payment cap rule. It says 340B claims must follow NYS Medicaid policy, and when Medicaid is the secondary payor, providers must follow the Pharmacy Manual for claim requirements. That makes the policy guidance broader and less specific on this point.

The Medicaid Update does not give a direct Part D enrollment or coverage rule for dual eligibles. It only points secondary-payor pharmacy claims back to the Pharmacy Manual for requirements. So the policy source is again indirect rather than a full parallel rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For dual eligibles, the total Medicare and Medicaid payment to the pharmacy cannot exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full.

Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plan or its exception and appeals process for needed prescriptions.
similarities: Both sources treat dual-eligible pharmacy billing as subject to separate Medicaid coordination rules rather than a simple single-payor claim path [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources direct secondary-payor situations back to the Pharmacy Manual for operational claim requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct payment-cap rule for dual eligibles, while the Medicaid Update mainly addresses 340B claims and secondary-payor routing instead of a general dual-eligible cap rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual specifically says full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in Medicare Part D and that Part D drugs are not covered by Medicaid, while the Medicaid Update does not state that rule directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
comparison: Both sources treat dual-eligible pharmacy billing as subject to separate Medicaid coordination rules rather than a simple single-payor claim path [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources direct secondary-payor situations back to the Pharmacy Manual for operational claim requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual gives a direct payment-cap rule for dual eligibles, while the Medicaid Update mainly addresses 340B claims and secondary-payor routing instead of a general dual-eligible cap rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual specifically says full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in Medicare Part D and that Part D drugs are not covered by Medicaid, while the Medicaid Update does not state that rule directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy chunk is only a partial match, so it does not supply a full standalone dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule. The strongest comparison is therefore Medicare-secondary and Part D coordination rather than all dual-eligible claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_2.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the provider manual says Medicaid is secondary to Medicare and will not pay more than the Medicaid-only amount. Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full, and Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be collected from dual eligibles.
answers.policy_definition: The policy update is narrower and focuses on NCPDP-submitted 340B drug claims when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor. Those claims must include claim-level identifiers and be billed at actual acquisition cost. For secondary-payor situations, the update directs providers back to the Pharmacy Manual requirements.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made. Any Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full, and Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be recovered from dual eligibles.
similarities: Both sources address how Medicaid payment rules operate in pharmacy claims where another payer may also be involved [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual states the general dual-eligible payment limit and collection rule, while the policy update is narrower and speaks to 340B primary-payor billing requirements instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
comparison: Both sources address how Medicaid payment rules operate in pharmacy claims where another payer may also be involved [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual states the general dual-eligible payment limit and collection rule, while the policy update is narrower and speaks to 340B primary-payor billing requirements instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
answers.caveats: The policy source in this comparison is partial for the dual-eligible topic because it mainly addresses 340B billing, so it does not restate the provider manuals full dual-eligible payment rule.

This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_2.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_2.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_3.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says dual eligible pharmacy claims are generally tied to Medicare first. Medicare payment plus any Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, and Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from dual eligibles. For Part D, full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan, and Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part D. The policy material largely reinforces Medicare-primary handling for dual-eligible claims, but the directly matched policy chunk is narrower and focused on 340B claim submission rules rather than general dual-eligible pharmacy claim processing.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid says 340B drug claims for Medicaid members must follow Pharmacy Manual policy and, when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, must be submitted at actual acquisition cost with claim-level identifiers. This is a narrower rule than the provider manual because it addresses 340B claim submission rather than the general dual-eligible payment limit.

The Medicaid Update requires 340B claims for Medicaid members to comply with Pharmacy Manual policy and to use claim-level identifiers when NYS Medicaid is the primary payor. This is broader only in the sense that it points back to manual policy, but it does not address Medicare enrollment, assignment, or Part D coverage rules.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, total Medicare and Medicaid payment to the pharmacy cannot exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full, and Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from the member.

Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies may need Medicare enrollment depending on their business model. The manual also says full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan, and Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part.
similarities: Both sources tie pharmacy billing for Medicaid members to specific program rules rather than leaving payment handling to standard claim submission alone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources require pharmacy providers to follow claim-level requirements when the Medicaid program is involved, although the policy update focuses on 340B identifiers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual gives the direct dual-eligible payment limit, while the policy update is narrower and only addresses 340B claim submission rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual also addresses Medicare assignment, Medicare enrollment, and Part D coverage for full-benefit dual eligibles, but the policy update does not discuss those topics [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
comparison: Both sources tie pharmacy billing for Medicaid members to specific program rules rather than leaving payment handling to standard claim submission alone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources require pharmacy providers to follow claim-level requirements when the Medicaid program is involved, although the policy update focuses on 340B identifiers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual gives the direct dual-eligible payment limit, while the policy update is narrower and only addresses 340B claim submission rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual also addresses Medicare assignment, Medicare enrollment, and Part D coverage for full-benefit dual eligibles, but the policy update does not discuss those topics [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
answers.caveats: The matched policy source is only a partial comparison because it focuses on 340B claims rather than general dual-eligible pharmacy claim processing.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_4.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the provider manual says Medicare Part D rules control coverage for full-benefit dual eligibles. Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Part D, and combined Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed what Medicaid-only would pay.
answers.policy_definition: The policy update addresses a different dual-eligible pharmacy scenario by requiring 340B claims to follow NYS Medicaid policy and Medicaid Update articles. When NYS Medicaid is the primary payor, those 340B claims must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. The policy text is broader on 340B billing and does not state the Part D coverage rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, the member must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Combined Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount.
similarities: Both sources address how Medicaid interacts with pharmacy claims for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources require pharmacies to follow NYS Medicaid rules when the claim is handled under the Medicaid program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual gives the Part D coverage rule for full-benefit dual eligibles, while the policy update instead focuses on 340B claim submission details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual says Medicaid does not cover Part D drugs and limits total payment to the Medicaid-only amount, but the policy update says 340B claims must include claim level identifiers and actual acquisition cost when Medicaid is primary payor [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources require pharmacies to follow NYS Medicaid rules when the claim is handled under the Medicaid program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

test_id_4.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_4.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_4.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_5.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the provider manual says Medicare is primary and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount. It also says full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan, because Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Part.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy article is narrower, but it points pharmacies to Medicaid policy and the pharmacy manual when Medicaid is the secondary payor for 340B claims. It does not restate the dual-eligible payment cap, but it supports manual control over secondary-payor pharmacy billing rules.

The policy article does not directly restate the Medicare assignment rule. It only confirms that pharmacies should follow the pharmacy manual for related dual-payor claim requirements, so the policy guidance is broader and less specific.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, the total payment to the pharmacy cannot exceed the amount that would have been paid for a Medicaid-only patient. If Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, Medicaid makes no additional payment, and the combined payment must be accepted as payment in full.

Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignment for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
similarities: Both sources place dual-payor pharmacy billing under Medicaid policy and manual guidance when Medicaid is not the only payer [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources treat the pharmacy manual as the controlling source for the more detailed claim submission rules [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual gives the specific dual-eligible payment limit and payment-in-full rule, while the policy article only points to manual guidance for secondary-payor claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual also adds the Medicare Part D enrollment rule for full-benefit dual eligibles, but the policy article in the retrieved text does not address Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both sources place dual-payor pharmacy billing under Medicaid policy and manual guidance when Medicaid is not the only payer [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources treat the pharmacy manual as the controlling source for the more detailed claim submission rules [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual gives the specific dual-eligible payment limit and payment-in-full rule, while the policy article only points to manual guidance for secondary-payor claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual also adds the Medicare Part D enrollment rule for full-benefit dual eligibles, but the policy article in the retrieved text does not address Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy article is only a partial match because it discusses 340B and secondary-payor processing rather than dual-eligible pharmacy claims directly.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.

dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

test_id_6.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort, and pharmacies must bill Medicare or any other responsible coverage first before billing NYS Medicaid. The newer pharmacy manual also says full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in Medicare Part D, and Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. The policy also says providers must submit claims under the appropriate coverage benefit, including pharmacy coverage where applicable. It is broader than the manual because it does not specifically name Medicare Part.

NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, because Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies are responsible for coordinating with the proper primary payer before sending the claim to NYS Medicaid. The policy is consistent with the manual but is less specific about the payment in full rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must use the Part D plan or its exception and appeals process for needed drugs.

Pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage per that plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. Bypassing responsible third-party payment is considered fraudulent billing.
similarities: Both sources require the pharmacy to pursue other available coverage before billing NYS Medicaid.
Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to a responsible primary payer for the claim.
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it names Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligibles and says those drugs are not covered by Medicaid. The policy update gives the broader coordination of benefits rule without naming Part D. [New York State Medicaid Program:

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_6.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."

test_id_7.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_7.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the provider manual says Medicaid is not primary when Medicare Part D covers the drug class. The member must be enrolled in a Part D plan, and Medicaid does not cover Part D-covered classes for full-benefit dual eligible members.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid requires providers and pharmacies to bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid. The policy is broader than the provider manual because it applies to all liable coverage, not only Medicare Part D. Pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer, including the pharmacy benefit when applicable.

NYS Medicaid says providers must bill all available third parties first, and Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must use coordination of benefits before Medicaid will consider payment. The policy also says Medicaid pays the patient responsibility for correctly submitted coverable claims, up to the allowed amount.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, the member must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plan, including its exception and appeals process, for needed drugs that are not on the formulary.

Pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Bypassing responsible third-party payment is fraudulent billing and is subject to audit. The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to the proper primary payer for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific dual-eligible rule tied to Medicare Part D coverage, while the policy update states the broader coordination of benefits rule for all liable third parties [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to the proper primary payer for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual gives a specific dual-eligible rule tied to Medicare Part D coverage, while the policy update states the broader coordination of benefits rule for all liable third parties [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort. The provider manual also says dual-eligible members must be enrolled in Medicare Part D for prescription coverage, and Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part D for full-benefit dual eligibles.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid requires providers and pharmacies to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must submit the claim to the proper primary payer and exhaust other coverage first.

similarities: Both sources make Medicaid secondary to other available coverage for dual-eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds the payment cap and payment-in-full rule, while the policy update is broader and focuses on coordination of benefits and the primary payer sequence [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual also states that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in Medicare Part D for prescription coverage and that Medicaid does not cover Part D drugs for full-benefit dual eligibles, which the policy update does not address [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
