test_id_9.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_9.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must bill Medicare or other applicable third parties first, and Medicaid comes after the primary payer has been used.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, and pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer. The policy is broader and does not specifically name Part D in this passage. It also requires submission under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage first and must coordinate with the proper primary payer. The policy also says to submit under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicare members who also have Medicaid must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any drug class covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find a covered Part D option or use the plans exception and appeals process.

Pharmacies must submit claims to a members other coverage before billing Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and a claim may be resubmitted to Medicaid only after proper third-party adjudication. The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
similarities: Both sources say other coverage must be billed before Medicaid for dual-eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary and require coordination with the proper primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds a Part D-specific rule for full-benefit dual eligibles, while the policy update gives a broader coordination-of-benefits rule without naming Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual states that Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full and that resubmission can follow proper third-party adjudication, while the policy update does not include those operational details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources say other coverage must be billed before Medicaid for dual-eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary and require coordination with the proper primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual adds a Part D-specific rule for full-benefit dual eligibles, while the policy update gives a broader coordination-of-benefits rule without naming Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual states that Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full and that resubmission can follow proper third-party adjudication, while the policy update does not include those operational details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

test_id_10.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_10.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, NYS Medicaid says other available coverage must be billed first, and Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The pharmacy manual adds that Medicare Part D is the primary prescription-drug coverage for full-benefit dual eligibles, and Medicare or Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid requires providers and pharmacies to bill applicable liable third parties before billing Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, so pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer. The policy is broader because it addresses all third-party coverage, not only Medicare Part.

Providers must exhaust all existing benefits before billing NYS Medicaid, and pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage before submitting claims to Medicaid. The policy also says pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer. This is less specific than the manual because it does not single out Part D dual eligibles.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, Medicare Part D prescription coverage must be in place to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Any Medicare or Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.

Pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage before submitting the claim to Medicaid. If the claim is not properly adjudicated with the third-party liability payer, the pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid. Submitting around the responsible payer is treated as fraudulent billing.
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid pays for a claim [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary or last-resort coverage for these claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The pharmacy manual is more specific because it says Medicare Part D is the primary prescription-drug coverage for full-benefit dual eligibles and that drugs covered under Part D are not covered by Medicaid for those members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it applies to all liable third parties and all existing benefits before Medicaid is billed, rather than naming Part D as the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be used before Medicaid pays for a claim [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary or last-resort coverage for these claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The pharmacy manual is more specific because it says Medicare Part D is the primary prescription-drug coverage for full-benefit dual eligibles and that drugs covered under Part D are not covered by Medicaid for those members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it applies to all liable third parties and all existing benefits before Medicaid is billed, rather than naming Part D as the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

test_id_10.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual is more explicit and current. For dual-eligible members, providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full and may not collect Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member. The policy is broader and older, mainly saying Medicare is billed first and Medicaid pays Part B cost sharing only to the extent allowed by law.
answers.policy_definition:
Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so Medicare must be billed first. For dually enrolled Medicare and Medicaid members, Medicaid will reimburse Part B coinsurance and deductible only to the extent permitted by New York State law. This is broader and less specific than the manual’s direct prohibition on collection from the member.

The policy update does not give the same payment-cap detail. It only reinforces that duals are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid pays after other sources are exhausted. That makes it a general coordination-of-benefits statement rather than a direct billing instruction.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, the pharmacy provider must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, as payment in full. The provider cannot recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the dual-eligible member. This is the current operational rule for pharmacy billing.

The manual also states that the total Medicare and Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made. The member still cannot be billed for the Part B deductible or coinsurance.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two:

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual is more specific and current. For Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible members, the manual says payment must be accepted as payment in full, and Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from the member.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update says dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules. It does not spell out pharmacy copayment or deductible collection from the member. The policy is therefore broader and less specific on this point.

Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so Medicare and other reimbursement sources must be billed before Medicaid. The policy is broader than the manual because it focuses on billing order rather than pharmacy-specific dual-eligible coverage limits.

This is the clearest current pharmacy rule in the manual.

For dual-eligible members, Medicaid is limited to certain drugs on the Medicaid reimbursable list when the drug class is excluded from Medicare Part D. Claims submitted to Medicaid are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual says Medicare and Medicaid payments for dual-eligible members must be accepted as payment in full, and pharmacies cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from those members. The policy update is broader and less pharmacy-specific because it says duals are subject to Medicare rules, while the manual gives the clearer current instruction on cost-sharing collection.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update says dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules. It does not specifically address pharmacy collection of Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual-eligible members. The policy statement is therefore broader and less specific.

The Medicaid Update does not give a dual-eligible-specific copay waiver rule. It only reinforces the broader point that duals are subject to Medicare rules. That makes it less direct than the manual on copayment responsibility.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals, the payment must be accepted as payment in full. The pharmacy provider cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from those individuals. The manual also ties this rule to Medicare Part B pharmacy billing.

Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only on a case-by-case basis for financial need at the request of the individual member or enrollee. The manual states this as the current operational rule for Medicaid copay collection.
similarities: Both sources recognize that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare-related billing rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources support the general idea that provider payment and member cost-sharing are limited by program rules rather than by routine provider billing practice [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific pharmacy rule that Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be collected from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals, while the policy update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual also states that copays cannot be proactively waived and may be waived only case by case for financial need, while the policy update does not provide that copay-waiver instruction [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
comparison: Both sources recognize that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare-related billing rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Both sources support the general idea that provider payment and member cost-sharing are limited by program rules rather than by routine provider billing practice [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual gives a specific pharmacy rule that Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be collected from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals, while the policy update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual also states that copays cannot be proactively waived and may be waived only case by case for financial need, while the policy update does not provide that copay-waiver instruction [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
answers.caveats: The policy source in this comparison is a brief update that points readers to broader Medicare-rule changes, so it is less detailed than the provider manual on dual-eligible copayment responsibility.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_13.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."

It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider-manual guidance says Medicare and Medicaid payments for dual-eligible members cannot be used to charge the member Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. The policy update is only partly aligned because it says duals are subject to Medicare rules, but it does not specifically address pharmacy co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It does not specifically address pharmacy copays or member liability for Medicare Part B cost-sharing.

The policy update does not add a dual-eligible-specific copay waiver rule. It is broader and only points to Medicare-rule applicability for duals.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, the pharmacy cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full.

Providers cannot proactively waive Medicaid copays. Waivers are allowed only case by case based on financial need and at the members request.
similarities: Both sources recognize that dual-eligible members are governed by Medicare-related billing rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct pharmacy rule that dual-eligible members cannot be billed Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts, while the Medicaid Update only states that duals are subject to Medicare rules and does not specifically address that cost-sharing issue [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual also states a general Medicaid copay waiver rule, but the policy update does not provide a separate dual-eligible copay waiver instruction [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
comparison: Both sources recognize that dual-eligible members are governed by Medicare-related billing rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual gives a direct pharmacy rule that dual-eligible members cannot be billed Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts, while the Medicaid Update only states that duals are subject to Medicare rules and does not specifically address that cost-sharing issue [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual also states a general Medicaid copay waiver rule, but the policy update does not provide a separate dual-eligible copay waiver instruction [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
answers.caveats: The policy source is only a partial match for this question because it does not speak directly to pharmacy co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.

test_id_15.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual gives a dual-eligible-specific rule: Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, and the pharmacy cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member. The policy update is broader and less specific because it says duals are subject to Medicare rules, but it does not spell out that same prohibition on recovering Part B cost-sharing.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It does not state a dual-eligible-specific prohibition on recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance. So it is related, but it is less specific on this point.

The policy update does not address copay waiver mechanics directly. It only notes that duals are subject to Medicare rules. So it does not give the operational waiver standard found in the manual.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals, the pharmacy must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full. The pharmacy cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member. The manual ties this rule to Medicare Part B pharmacy billing.

The provider manual separately says providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only case by case based on financial need and at the members request. That is a general Medicaid copay rule, not a dual-eligible exception.
similarities: Both sources treat dual-eligible members as being governed by Medicare-related billing rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

Both sources point to limits on what providers may collect from members, although they do so at different levels of detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance recovery from dual-eligible individuals, while the policy update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual also gives the operational copay waiver standard, but the policy update does not address waiver mechanics directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
comparison: Both sources treat dual-eligible members as being governed by Medicare-related billing rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

Both sources point to limits on what providers may collect from members, although they do so at different levels of detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual gives a specific rule for Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance recovery from dual-eligible individuals, while the policy update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

The provider manual also gives the operational copay waiver standard, but the policy update does not address waiver mechanics directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
answers.caveats: The policy source in this comparison is broader than the provider manual and is not as specific on dual-eligible member cost-sharing.

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

test_id_15.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_15.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_15.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members unless the drug is legally excluded from Part D. The older Medicaid Update policy reaches the same practical result for dual eligibles and points members and prescribers to the Part D coverage, exception, and appeals process.
answers.policy_definition:
For dual eligibles, Medicaid does not cover any drug class covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers should look for a Part D-covered alternative or use the Part D exception and appeals process for needed drugs not on the Part D formulary. This policy is broader and less specific than the provider manual rule.

The policy says needed drugs that are not on the Part D formulary should go through the Part D plans exception and appeals process. It does not restate Medicaid billing or prior authorization rules. It is therefore a narrower workflow statement than the provider manual guidance.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid should not be billed for a prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug is specifically excluded from Part D by law. Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid reimbursable list that Part D cannot cover, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. This is the current billing rule for full-benefit dual eligible members.

Dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Claims submitted to Medicaid are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization. The manual also says Medicaid payment must stay within the Medicaid rules that apply to the claim.

Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.

All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

test_id_16.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_16.pm_ground_truth: Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_16.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for Medicare Part D member drugs unless the drug is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. The older Medicaid Update instead points members and prescribers to the Part D plan exception and appeals process first for needed drugs that are not on the plan.
answers.policy_definition: The policy update is broader and less specific on this point. It says dual-eligible members and prescribers should work with the Part D plans formulary exception and appeals process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan. It does not state the current Medicaid payment rule for Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.provider_manual_definition: Medicaid should not be billed for a Medicare Part D members prescription drug unless the drug is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. Medicaid only pays for List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs items that are Part D-excluded, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All dual-eligible claims submitted to Medicaid remain subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
similarities: Both sources address how to handle prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members or dual-eligible members.
differences: The provider manual gives the current Medicaid billing rule for Part D-excluded drugs, while the Medicaid Update focuses on using the Part D exception and appeals process first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources address how to handle prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members or dual-eligible members.

The provider manual gives the current Medicaid billing rule for Part D-excluded drugs, while the Medicaid Update focuses on using the Part D exception and appeals process first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
answers.caveats: The policy source is a partial match because it discusses the Part D-first exception process rather than the specific Medicaid rule for Part D-excluded drugs.

recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

test_id_18.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members, except for Medicaid-reimbursable drugs whose class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. The older Medicaid policy article is broader and does not specifically restate that exception, but it reinforces that Part D-covered drugs should go through Medicare first.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy article does not specifically restate the Part D-excluded-drug rule. It instead says that practitioner administered drugs billed directly to NYRx must be on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, and it is focused on practitioner administered drugs. That makes it broader and less specific than the manual on this point.

The policy article says that when a member has Medicare, providers bill Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plan for other prescription drugs. It also says that if the member is not enrolled in Part D, Part D-covered claims go to LINET to start Part D enrollment and help payment. That is a related billing pathway, but it is less direct than the manuals current Medicaid rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid does not pay for prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members unless the drug is on the Medicaid reimbursable list and the class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. Examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All dual-eligible claims submitted to Medicaid remain subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For full-benefit dual eligibles, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers should use the Part D plan or its exception and appeals process for drugs not on the formulary.
similarities: Both sources tie Medicaid pharmacy billing to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and Medicare coverage rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

Both sources show that drugs covered under Medicare Part D should not simply be billed to Medicaid first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual gives the current exception for drugs that are specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, while the policy article is older and only indirectly supports the rule through practitioner administered drug billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources tie Medicaid pharmacy billing to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and Medicare coverage rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

Both sources show that drugs covered under Medicare Part D should not simply be billed to Medicaid first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual gives the current exception for drugs that are specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, while the policy article is older and only indirectly supports the rule through practitioner administered drug billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
answers.caveats: The policy article is only a partial match because it focuses on practitioner administered drugs and does not directly restate the Part D-excluded-drug exception.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
