test_id_19.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for Medicare Part D drugs for dual-eligible members, except for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list that are specifically excluded from Part D by law. The older Medicaid Update is directionally consistent, but it is narrower and focuses more on using Part D coverage, exceptions, and appeals.
answers.policy_definition:
The Medicaid Update is narrower on this point and does not state the same exclusion list. It says full-benefit dual eligibles should use Medicare Part D for covered drugs and should use the Part D exception and appeals process if a needed drug is not on the formulary. That is related guidance, but it is less specific about the Medicaid payable exception.

The Medicaid Update also points dual eligibles to Medicare Part D rather than Medicaid for covered drugs. It says the member and prescriber should use the Part D exception and appeals process when a needed drug is not on the formulary. The policy is broader and does not restate the provider manuals current Medicaid payment exclusion as directly.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid generally should not be billed for a Medicare Part D members prescription drug. Medicaid only pays for drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. Claims for dual-eligible members are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any drug class covered under Medicare Part D. Members and their prescribers should find a Part D-covered alternative or use the Part D exception and appeals process for needed prescriptions not listed on the formulary. This is a direct current rule for billing and coverage decisions.
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare Part D as the primary payer pathway for dual-eligible members when the drug is Part D covered [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

Both sources point members and prescribers to the Part D exception and appeals process when a needed drug is not available on the Part D formulary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual gives the current Medicaid payment exception for drugs specifically excluded from Part D, while the Medicaid Update mainly describes the Part D workflow and does not spell out that exception in the same way [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources treat Medicare Part D as the primary payer pathway for dual-eligible members when the drug is Part D covered [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

Both sources point members and prescribers to the Part D exception and appeals process when a needed drug is not available on the Part D formulary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

The provider manual gives the current Medicaid payment exception for drugs specifically excluded from Part D, while the Medicaid Update mainly describes the Part D workflow and does not spell out that exception in the same way [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10].

test_id_20.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs for Medicare Part D dual-eligible members unless the drug is specifically excluded from Part D by law, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. The older Medicaid Update policy points in the same direction, but it is narrower and focuses on pharmacy billing for Medicaid-reimbursable drugs rather than the Part D exclusion rule itself.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy says certain practitioner administered drugs may be billed by a pharmacy only when they are on the Medicaid reimbursable list. It is a narrower billing guidance and does not directly state the Part D-excluded-drug exception. It is about proper dispensing and delivery of those drugs.

The policy does not directly address Part D covered drugs. It only says that pharmacy billing is limited to Medicaid reimbursable practitioner administered drugs. That means it supports the same Medicaid boundary, but it is less specific.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid generally should not be billed for a Medicare Part D members prescription drug unless the drug is on the Medicaid reimbursable list and is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. Select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs are examples. Dual-eligible claims are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers must use a Part D covered alternative or the Part D exception and appeals process when needed. The rule keeps the pharmacy claim outside Medicaid when Part D covers the drug.

The provider manual directly states the Part D exclusion rule for dual eligible members, while the policy update only addresses pharmacy billing for practitioner administered drugs and is broader, less specific guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program:
answers.caveats: The policy update is a partial match, so it should be read as supporting the billing framework rather than as a direct Part D exclusion rule.

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_21.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Under the current pharmacy manual, a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy must bill Medicare first for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, and it may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received. The policy update uses the same basic payer-order rule and says Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage first because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The policy statement is broader and does not give Medicare-specific billing instructions.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare. Medicaid can be billed only after Medicare payment information is received. The manual also says the pharmacy must bill the appropriate Medicare benefit and follow Medicare billing procedures.
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before NYS Medicaid when it exists [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The pharmacy manual is more specific because it names Medicare and says the pharmacy must bill the appropriate Medicare benefit and follow Medicare billing procedures. The policy update is broader and only addresses third-party billing in general [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before NYS Medicaid when it exists [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The pharmacy manual is more specific because it names Medicare and says the pharmacy must bill the appropriate Medicare benefit and follow Medicare billing procedures. The policy update is broader and only addresses third-party billing in general [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_21.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_21.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_21.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_22.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Current provider-manual guidance says a pharmacy enrolled as a Medicaid billing provider must bill Medicare first for services covered by both programs, and Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received. The policy update states the same general payer-of-last-resort rule, while the provider manual is more specific about the Medicare billing sequence.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid because Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage first and coordinate with the proper primary payer. This policy statement is broader than the provider manual because it does not spell out the Medicare sequence.
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid as billing providers must maximize Medicare before billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Medicaid may be billed only after payment information is received from Medicare.
similarities: Both sources require another payer to be billed before NYS Medicaid for a pharmacy claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage for coordination of benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual gives the Medicare-specific workflow and says Medicaid can be billed only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy update states the broader third-party liability rule without that operational detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources require another payer to be billed before NYS Medicaid for a pharmacy claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_22.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_22.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_22.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_23.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_23.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Under the current provider manual, pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers must bill Medicare first for services covered by both programs, and they may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received. The policy update says the same basic rule: pharmacies must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid requires providers to bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid. Pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage first because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The policy is broader and does not focus only on Medicare-covered pharmacy claims.

Providers must exhaust all existing benefits before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before Medicaid and for coordinating with the proper primary payer. The policy is broader and mentions coordination of benefits at a high level rather than the manuals resubmission details.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid as billing providers must bill Medicare first when a service is covered by both Medicare and Medicaid. They may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received. The manual also says they must use the Medicare benefit and billing method that the Medicare plan requires.

Pharmacies must submit claims to a member's other coverage according to that plan's requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. If the issue is resolved, they may resubmit to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the third-party liability plan. The manual also warns that bypassing the responsible third party is fraudulent billing.
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before NYS Medicaid when another payer is responsible [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort and place the billing sequence on the pharmacy or provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it says pharmacies may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy update states the broader third-party liability rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual also adds Medicare-specific billing instructions, including using the Medicare benefit and required billing code pathway, while the policy update does not reach that level of detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before NYS Medicaid when another payer is responsible [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort and place the billing sequence on the pharmacy or provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual is more specific because it says pharmacies may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy update states the broader third-party liability rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual also adds Medicare-specific billing instructions, including using the Medicare benefit and required billing code pathway, while the policy update does not reach that level of detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it speaks to all liable third parties and coordination of benefits, rather than giving a Medicare-first pharmacy workflow [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual is more specific because it says services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare, and Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it speaks to all liable third parties and coordination of benefits, rather than giving a Medicare-first pharmacy workflow [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_24.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_24.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_24.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_25.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_25.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Under the current provider manual, a pharmacy enrolled as a Medicaid billing provider must bill Medicare first for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, and it may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received. The April 2025 Medicaid Update says the same general coordination of benefits rule applies: pharmacies must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, which is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid requires pharmacies to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. The Update says Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so other available coverage must be used first. It is broader than the manual because it does not focus only on Medicare-covered pharmacy claims.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For pharmacy claims covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare. The pharmacy may bill Medicaid only after Medicare payment information is received. The manual also says the pharmacy may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures and must use the Medicare-required billing method.
similarities: Both sources require another payer to be billed before NYS Medicaid when other coverage is available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort for covered pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it requires Medicare to be billed first for claims covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, while the Medicaid Update states the broader third-party coordination rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual adds a Medicare billing procedure requirement, including using the Medicare plans required HCPCS or National Drug Code method, and the Medicaid Update does not include that operational detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both sources require another payer to be billed before NYS Medicaid when other coverage is available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort for covered pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual is more specific because it requires Medicare to be billed first for claims covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, while the Medicaid Update states the broader third-party coordination rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual adds a Medicare billing procedure requirement, including using the Medicare plans required HCPCS or National Drug Code method, and the Medicaid Update does not include that operational detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

test_id_26.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_26.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required after Medicare has approved and paid the service or procedure, even if Medicaid would otherwise have required prior approval. The older Medicaid Update also does not add a separate prior-approval requirement for crossover claims after Medicare payment.
answers.policy_definition:
Providers must bill other coverage first, and the policy says the responsible third party must pay first. It does not add a separate prior-approval requirement for crossover claims after Medicare payment. It is broader because it discusses other coverage generally, not just crossover claims.

Federal and Medicaid rules require providers to use all available resources before Medicaid considers payment. The policy says the responsible third party must pay first, and it also states that claims with other coverage deny at point of service with code 717. This matches the general billing order, but it is not limited to crossover claims.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and or procedure. That rule still applies even when the procedure would otherwise have required Medicaid prior approval. This is the current provider manual guidance for crossover claims.

Medicaid is payer of last resort, so providers must seek any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. The provider manual says the responsible third party must pay first. It also notes that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the primary payors responsibility.
similarities: Both sources say other coverage must be billed before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
Both sources say the responsible third party must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual specifically says prior approval is not required on Medicaid or Medicare crossover claims after Medicare has approved and paid the service or procedure, while the Medicaid Update only addresses the broader other-coverage billing rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
comparison: Both sources say other coverage must be billed before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
Both sources say the responsible third party must pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The provider manual specifically says prior approval is not required on Medicaid or Medicare crossover claims after Medicare has approved and paid the service or procedure, while the Medicaid Update only addresses the broader other-coverage billing rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.
