test_id_36.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

test_id_37.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_37.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist does not have to add the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN if the information is already available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The pharmacist also has a separate duty to use professional judgment when prescriber verification is unavailable in certain emergency-related situations.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy is broader and does not address missing member identifiers. It instead says the pharmacy must use professional judgment for prescriptions written during an emergency department visit when the assigned PCP is unavailable to verify them. The pharmacy must contact the assigned PCP the next business day.

The policy is related, but it is less specific about identity verification. It says providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. It does not restate the manual’s good faith effort standard for an unknown prescriber.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill. The pharmacist also does not have to enter it if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. Controlled substance prescriptions still must meet Article 33 requirements, and only certain information may be added or changed.

If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. This is an affirmative responsibility before the prescription is filled. The manual also notes that controlled substance prescriptions have separate legal limits on what may be changed.

differences: The provider manual focuses on missing member information and prescriber identification, while the policy focuses on emergency department verification and next-business-day PCP contact. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines;
comparison: Both sources require the pharmacist or pharmacy to use judgment before filling when information or verification is missing. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines;

The provider manual focuses on missing member information and prescriber identification, while the policy focuses on emergency department verification and next-business-day PCP contact. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines;

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_38.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_38.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, pharmacists do not have to write it onto the prescription if it is already readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For prescriber-related gaps, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription and the prescriber’s identity, and should contact the prescriber as needed when details must be verified or changed.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy is broader and does not give a patient-identification rule. It instead says the pharmacy should use professional judgment in emergency department prescriptions when the assigned PCP is unavailable after hours. It also requires contact with the assigned PCP the next business day for those prescriptions.

The policy is closely related but less specific. It says providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. It does not state the same good-faith identity-verification rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it on the prescription when the information is already readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill. The pharmacist also does not have to enter it when otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. This is a limited exception for those missing patient-identification items.

The pharmacist is responsible for making a good-faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. The manual frames this as an affirmative verification duty. It applies when the prescriber is not known.

answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it on the prescription in two limited situations. The information must already be readily available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist must know the person requesting the fill, or the pharmacist must otherwise be satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. Controlled substance prescriptions remain subject to Article 33 and Title 10 requirements.

If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. The rule is framed as a general verification duty for unknown prescribers. It does not give a separate workflow for emergency department prescriptions.
similarities: Both sources require the pharmacist to act when prescription information is incomplete or uncertain [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources place the responsibility on the pharmacist or pharmacy to verify or review the prescription rather than simply fill it without question [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifically allows omission of missing member address, age, or CIN when the stated conditions are met, while the policy only gives a general direction to verify directions for use and consult the prescriber as needed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual gives a general rule for an unknown prescriber and requires a good faith effort to verify identity, while the policy uses an emergency department example that requires professional judgment and next-business-day follow-up [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources require the pharmacist to act when prescription information is incomplete or uncertain [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

Both sources place the responsibility on the pharmacist or pharmacy to verify or review the prescription rather than simply fill it without question [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual gives a general rule for an unknown prescriber and requires a good faith effort to verify identity, while the policy uses an emergency department example that requires professional judgment and next-business-day follow-up [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.

test_id_39.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

test_id_40.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist does not have to enter the missing address, age, or CIN if that information is already available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. If the prescriber is unknown, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy is broader and less specific than the manual. It says the pharmacy must use professional judgment in an emergency department situation when the assigned primary care provider is unavailable, and it must contact that provider the next business day. It does not state the same missing-member-information rule.

The policy is broader and less specific than the manual. It says providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. It does not specifically address an unknown prescriber.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it when the information is already readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester. The pharmacist is also not required to enter it when the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. For controlled substances, the prescription must still meet Article 33 requirements, and only certain information may be added or changed.

If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist is responsible for making a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity. This is an affirmative verification duty when the prescriber cannot be readily identified.
similarities: Both sources require the pharmacist to use judgment and take follow-up action when prescription information is not fully available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources point to verification duties rather than automatic rejection when key prescription information needs confirmation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The manual gives a specific rule for missing member address, age, or CIN, while the policy instead gives an emergency department example that relies on professional judgment and next-business-day follow-up [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The manual specifically requires a good-faith effort to verify an unknown prescriber’s identity, while the policy only says to verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
comparison: Both sources require the pharmacist to use judgment and take follow-up action when prescription information is not fully available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources point to verification duties rather than automatic rejection when key prescription information needs confirmation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The manual gives a specific rule for missing member address, age, or CIN, while the policy instead gives an emergency department example that relies on professional judgment and next-business-day follow-up [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The manual specifically requires a good-faith effort to verify an unknown prescriber’s identity, while the policy only says to verify directions and consult the prescriber as necessary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
answers.caveats: The policy sources are only partial matches, so the comparison reflects the closest related verification and follow-up duties rather than an exact duplicate of the manual rule.

test_id_41.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement, and the Pharmacy Manual also says compounds may not be made for excluded indications such as drugs to treat weight loss. The current provider manual guidance is the most up to date and confirms this exclusion.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. The policy statement is broader because it also frames the rule as an exclusion under SSA section 1927(d)(2).

Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. This matches the provider manual on the compounding restriction.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable.

Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications. That includes drugs to treat weight loss.
similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also treat weight loss as an excluded indication for compounded prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more operational because it adds the current compounding rule and ties reimbursement to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is older and states the exclusion more generally, while the provider manual gives the newer and more specific current guidance [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also treat weight loss as an excluded indication for compounded prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

The provider manual is more operational because it adds the current compounding rule and ties reimbursement to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is older and states the exclusion more generally, while the provider manual gives the newer and more specific current guidance [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_42.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_42.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid treats drugs used for weight loss as an excluded or nonreimbursable benefit when they are used for that purpose. The provider manual also says compounded drugs may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. The policy states the exclusion directly and more broadly for those drug uses. It is a general reimbursement rule rather than a compound-specific one.
answers.provider_manual_definition: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. That includes drugs to treat weight loss. The rule is stated as a compound restriction in the pharmacy manual.
similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not covered by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual frames the rule as a restriction on compounded drugs, while the policy update states a direct reimbursement exclusion for weight loss drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not covered by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual frames the rule as a restriction on compounded drugs, while the policy update states a direct reimbursement exclusion for weight loss drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement. The provider manual also says compounded products may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, and drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable. The update states the same exclusion in general coverage terms. It is broader than the provider manual because it does not focus on compounded claims.

Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The policy update says the same thing for compounded prescriptions. It matches the operational rule closely.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, and drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable. This is current provider guidance for pharmacy coverage. It applies as a reimbursement limitation unless another listed program rule says otherwise.

Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The compound must also meet the usual reimbursement and compendia-supported requirements. This means a compounded product cannot be used to bypass the weight loss exclusion.

Both sources also apply the exclusion to compounded products made for a weight loss indication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is newer and adds detailed compound claim limits, including the rule that ingredients must be on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and compendia-supported for the intended route and indication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The January 2025 update states the general reimbursement exclusion, but it is less specific about compound billing conditions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also apply the exclusion to compounded products made for a weight loss indication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_44.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_44.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid treats drugs used for weight loss as an excluded, non-reimbursable benefit. The provider manual also says compounds may not be made for excluded indications such as drugs to treat weight loss.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain under Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2). This is stated as a general drug coverage exclusion. It is broader than the compound-specific manual language.

NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs included on the New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. The policy also names drugs used for weight loss or weight gain as not reimbursable. This closely matches the manual, although the policy is stated more generally.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications under Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2). That includes drugs to treat weight loss. The manual also places this rule in the compound-claims context.

Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are listed as non-reimbursable examples. This frames weight-loss use as a coverage limitation, not a covered benefit.
similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources tie the exclusion to Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual states the rule in the compound-claim context, while the policy update states the broader non-reimbursable drug exclusion [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources tie the exclusion to Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual states the rule in the compound-claim context, while the policy update states the broader non-reimbursable drug exclusion [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

test_id_45.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_45.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid treats drugs used for weight loss as non-reimbursable and excluded. The provider manual also says compounded drugs may not be made for excluded indications such as weight loss.
answers.policy_definition:
Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss. The policy statement is the same rule, but it is slightly broader in its wording. It confirms that weight loss is excluded even when the drugs are part of a compound.

NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are examples of drugs that are not reimbursable under state plan coverage. The policy side states the same exclusion in broader coverage language.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. Ingredients in a compound claim are reimbursable only if they are otherwise allowed under Medicaid rules. This means weight-loss drugs cannot be used to make a payable compounded claim.

Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are listed as not reimbursable. This is a direct exclusion for standalone weight-loss drugs.
similarities: Both sources exclude drugs used for weight loss from Medicaid reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also tie the exclusion to Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual adds the operational detail that compounded claims cannot use excluded ingredients or excluded indications, while the update states the exclusion more generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
comparison: Both sources exclude drugs used for weight loss from Medicaid reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also tie the exclusion to Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual adds the operational detail that compounded claims cannot use excluded ingredients or excluded indications, while the update states the exclusion more generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications."

test_id_46.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: NY Medicaid reimburses eligible 340B purchased drugs at 340B acquisition cost, with a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 when the claim qualifies and is not paid at U&C. 340B claims must also be identified at submission level so NY Medicaid can avoid duplicate discounts and rebate invoicing issues.
answers.policy_definition:
340B drugs obtained through the program must be billed with the NDC, actual acquisition cost by invoice, and the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. That policy is narrower than the manual because it describes the required billing inputs rather than the full payment rule.

340B drug claims must be identified by appending the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. The policy also requires the NDC and other claim details, but it does not discuss the exclusion file.

Providers must include the number of units administered and the actual acquisition cost by invoice in the charges field for 340B drug reimbursement. That is related, but it is less specific than the manuals NCPDP field instructions.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
340B purchased drugs reimburse at 340B Acquisition Cost. If the claim qualifies as a 340B purchased drug claim, the professional dispensing fee is $10.18, and the claim pays U&C if U&C is lower.

NY Medicaid uses 340B claim-level identifiers, not HRSAs Medicaid Exclusion File, and these identifiers are required at claim submission. Using them keeps 340B claims out of rebate invoicing.

For Medicaid 340B drug claims in NCPDP format, the ingredient cost field must contain 340B acquisition cost. The U&C field must contain the lowest net charge to cash customers.
similarities: Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to the submitted acquisition cost for the claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources require claim-level identification of 340B drugs during billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The manual states the payment rule directly, including the $10.18 dispensing fee and the U&C override, while the policy focuses on billing elements such as the UD modifier and invoice-based acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual says NY Medicaid relies on claim-level identifiers instead of the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, but the policy does not mention the exclusion file and only instructs providers to append UD on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to the submitted acquisition cost for the claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources require claim-level identification of 340B drugs during billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual states the payment rule directly, including the $10.18 dispensing fee and the U&C override, while the policy focuses on billing elements such as the UD modifier and invoice-based acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual says NY Medicaid relies on claim-level identifiers instead of the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, but the policy does not mention the exclusion file and only instructs providers to append UD on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The policy source is older and more limited than the provider manual, so the manual controls the current payment description.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.

Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

test_id_46.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_46.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_46.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_47.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at 340B acquisition cost when the claim is eligible and properly identified as a 340B purchased drug claim. NY Medicaid also requires claim-level 340B identifiers and FFS reporting details to support that payment.
answers.policy_definition:
For NYS Medicaid FFS claims, 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier. The actual acquisition cost must be reported in the claim charges field for reimbursement. The policy is broader because it explains the FFS reporting steps rather than stating the payment formula alone.

340B drugs on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line. The policy is narrower here because it names the modifier requirement for FFS claims, but it does not describe the rebate invoicing process.

NYS Medicaid requires the NDC, the number of units administered, and the actual acquisition cost on 340B FFS claims. It will not reimburse if the required drug identification is missing. The policy is broader because it states required claim elements rather than the full NCPDP field set.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For 340B purchased drugs, Medicaid reimburses at 340B Acquisition Cost. The usual dispensing fee amount is $10.18 when the claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. The claim must qualify as a 340B purchased drug claim for that pricing to apply.

NY Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies on claim-level 340B identifiers for all 340B drug claims to avoid duplicate discounts and remove the claim from rebate invoicing. That identification is required at submission.

For Medicaid 340B drug claims, the NCPDP format requires SCC 20, BCD 08, and ingredient cost reported as 340B Acquisition Cost. The table also shows U&C as a separate field. The manual is specific about the billing fields used for 340B claims.
similarities: Both sources tie 340B payment to correct claim identification and reporting for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources require actual acquisition cost to be part of the 340B claim submission [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual states the reimbursement amount directly as 340B Acquisition Cost, while the policy update focuses on the UD modifier and FFS claim fields needed to support reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources tie 340B payment to correct claim identification and reporting for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources require actual acquisition cost to be part of the 340B claim submission [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual states the reimbursement amount directly as 340B Acquisition Cost, while the policy update focuses on the UD modifier and FFS claim fields needed to support reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
