provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."

test_id_48.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses eligible 340B purchased drugs at the 340B acquisition cost, with a $10.18 professional dispensing fee when applicable. The claim must be identified as a 340B drug claim.
answers.policy_definition:
For NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified with the UD modifier. Providers must also report the NDC and actual acquisition cost by invoice in the charges field. This policy is broader on claim reporting than on payment amount.

The policy says that 340B drugs on FFS-only claims must be marked with the UD modifier. It also requires the NDC and actual acquisition cost to be reported. That is a narrower operational instruction than the manual’s duplicate discount rule.

The policy requires the NDC for 340B drug reimbursement on FFS claims. It also requires the actual acquisition cost by invoice in the charges field and the UD modifier on the 340B drug line. It does not list the full NCPDP field set in the same level of detail.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For eligible 340B purchased drug claims, Medicaid reimburses at 340B Acquisition Cost. A $10.18 professional dispensing fee applies when the drug is not paid at U&C. This is the current pricing rule for eligible 340B purchased drug claims.

NY Medicaid does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies completely on 340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts and to remove the claim from rebate invoicing. Those identifiers are required on all 340B drug claims.

For Medicaid 340B drug claims in NCPDP format, the ingredient cost field must reflect 340B Acquisition Cost. The U&C field is still required. Submitting claim level identifiers on non-340B claims is fraudulent billing.
similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified before Medicaid will process them correctly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie payment to actual 340B acquisition cost rather than a generic drug price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The manual states the reimbursement amount and dispensing fee, while the policy update focuses on claim identifiers and reporting fields for FFS claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual says NY Medicaid relies on claim level identifiers instead of the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, but the policy update does not discuss the exclusion file and only gives the UD modifier and billing field requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified before Medicaid will process them correctly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie payment to actual 340B acquisition cost rather than a generic drug price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual states the reimbursement amount and dispensing fee, while the policy update focuses on claim identifiers and reporting fields for FFS claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual says NY Medicaid relies on claim level identifiers instead of the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, but the policy update does not discuss the exclusion file and only gives the UD modifier and billing field requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

test_id_49.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid reimburses eligible 340B purchased drugs at 340B acquisition cost, with a $10.18 professional dispensing fee when applicable. Payment is still limited by usual and customary pricing if that is lower.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy update says 340B drugs must be identified on NYS Medicaid fee-for-service claims with the UD modifier. It also requires the National Drug Code, units, and actual acquisition cost to be reported. That guidance is about claim submission for reimbursement, so it is broader and less specific on the final pricing rule.

The policy update requires the UD modifier on 340B drug lines for NYS Medicaid fee-for-service claims. It also requires the National Drug Code and actual acquisition cost to be reported. The policy is less specific than the manual about rebate invoicing and duplicate discount prevention.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For eligible 340B purchased drug claims, NY Medicaid reimburses at 340B Acquisition Cost. A $10.18 professional dispensing fee applies when the drug meets the definition of a covered outpatient drug and is not paid at usual and customary pricing. Claims pay at usual and customary pricing if it is lower than drug ingredient cost plus the dispensing fee.

NY Medicaid does not use HRSAs Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies completely on 340B claim-level identifiers submitted with the claim to avoid duplicate discounts. Those identifiers are the only way NY Medicaid removes the claim from rebate invoicing.

pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

test_id_50.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly identified as a 340B purchased drug claim. NY Medicaid uses the 340B pathway rather than the usual drug pricing formulas for these claims.
answers.policy_definition:
Drugs obtained through the 340B program must be identified on NYS Medicaid claims. The claim must include the actual acquisition cost so reimbursement is based on that submitted 340B cost.

340B drugs must be identified by appending the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. The claim must also report the NDC and the actual acquisition cost for reimbursement.

NY Medicaid required 340B claims to be properly identified and submitted with the 340B acquisition cost in the ingredient cost field. It also required the lowest net charge to cash customers in the U&C field.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For 340B purchased drugs, NY Medicaid reimburses at 340B Acquisition Cost. The usual dispensing fee of $10.18 applies if the claim is eligible for that fee.

NY Medicaid does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File for 340B claims. It relies on claim-level 340B identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts, and those identifiers are required on all 340B drug claims.

For NCPDP 340B drug claims, the required fields include SCC 20, BCD 08, ingredient cost as 340B Acquisition Cost, and U&C as lowest net charge to cash customers. These field rules apply to Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format.
similarities: Both sources say 340B reimbursement depends on identifying the claim as a 340B claim and using the 340B acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie correct 340B billing to claim-level identification so the claim is processed under the 340B pathway [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about the current reimbursement amount and lists the $10.18 dispensing fee, while the policy update mainly describes the required claim submission elements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual says NY Medicaid does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, while the policy update instead emphasizes the UD modifier and NDC reporting for FFS claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie correct 340B billing to claim-level identification so the claim is processed under the 340B pathway [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual says NY Medicaid does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File, while the policy update instead emphasizes the UD modifier and NDC reporting for FFS claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

test_id_51.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid does not pay a bundled home-infusion rate. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, depending on how the drug is covered.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, may be available through both the medical benefit and the pharmacy benefit if they meet pharmacy-benefit criteria. That policy statement is broader and focuses on eligibility criteria rather than a direct home-infusion billing rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition: New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual does not assign one bundled home-infusion billing method.
similarities: Both sources treat home infusion drugs as potentially payable outside a bundled home-infusion arrangement.
differences: The provider manual gives a direct billing rule for home infusion drugs and supplies, while the policy statement is broader and explains only when drugs may qualify for pharmacy-benefit billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both sources treat home infusion drugs as potentially payable outside a bundled home-infusion arrangement.

The provider manual gives a direct billing rule for home infusion drugs and supplies, while the policy statement is broader and explains only when drugs may qualify for pharmacy-benefit billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
answers.caveats: The policy evidence for this row is partial, and no separate policy chunk directly states the home-infusion billing rule.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion, and the drugs and supplies must be billed through either the pharmacy benefit or the medical benefit. The closest policy rule is broader and covers certain administered drugs, but it does not directly address home infusion billing responsibility.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid FFS covers certain drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods when they are FDA approved or otherwise medically accepted for a medically necessary covered service. Billing is subject to practitioner-administered drug guidance. This is related, but it is broader than home infusion coverage.

similarities: Both sources address coverage and billing for administered drugs under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual gives the direct home infusion rule and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy update only gives broader practitioner-administered drug coverage rules and does not address home infusion billing responsibility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives the direct home infusion rule and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy update only gives broader practitioner-administered drug coverage rules and does not address home infusion billing responsibility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_53.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_53.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and billing is handled on that basis rather than through one separate home-infusion payment.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy update is broader than home infusion, but it supports reimbursement for practitioner-administered drugs when coverage criteria are met. It also points providers to the practitioner-administered drug policies and billing guidance page for certain drug classes. It does not state a home-infusion-specific bundled payment rule.

The policy update says drugs given by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office are covered when they are FDA approved or otherwise medically accepted. It also says reimbursement follows the applicable practitioner-administered drug policies and billing guidance when the listed criteria are met. It does not address the home-infusion pharmacy enrollment rules in the manual.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Drugs and supplies associated with home infusion must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also points to the Medicaid reimbursable drug list for pharmacy benefit availability.

Home infusion drugs may be covered under either the pharmacy benefit or the medical benefit. Pharmacy coverage depends on meeting pharmacy billing criteria, including use of the NCPDP format and dispensing by a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy. The manual also requires the drug to be deliverable directly to the place of administration.
answers.caveats: The policy update is only a partial match because it addresses practitioner-administered drugs more broadly than home infusion. The provider manual is the more current source for the home-infusion billing rule.

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid does not pay a bundled rate for home infusion. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed either as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit.
answers.policy_definition:
Medicaid covers certain drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods when they meet FDA-approved or medically accepted indication requirements for a covered service. That policy is broader than the home infusion rule because it addresses practitioner-administered drugs generally, not home infusion billing specifically. It also adds that reimbursement depends on the related billing guidance being met.

The policy says injectable and infusion drugs are reimbursable when FDA-approved or medically accepted indication requirements are met and the other practitioner-administered drug billing guidance is satisfied. That statement is broader and does not list the pharmacy-benefit criteria from the manual. It does not say who bills in the home infusion setting.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, or services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual does not assign a single billing path for every case in this rule.

Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit when they meet the pharmacy-benefit criteria in the manual. The drug must be billable in NCPDP format, delivered to the place of administration, not included in a facility rate, and dispensed by a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy. The manual also requires pharmacist counseling and notes that review by the DUR Board may be needed in some cases.
similarities: Both sources say infusion-type drugs can be covered by New York State Medicaid when the applicable coverage and billing rules are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie payment to specific eligibility rules rather than to a single blanket payment rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual gives the home infusion billing pathway and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy is broader practitioner-administered drug guidance and does not address home infusion billing directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual lists pharmacy-benefit operational requirements such as NCPDP billing, direct delivery, Medicaid-enrolled dispensing, and counseling, but the policy does not [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources say infusion-type drugs can be covered by New York State Medicaid when the applicable coverage and billing rules are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie payment to specific eligibility rules rather than to a single blanket payment rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives the home infusion billing pathway and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy is broader practitioner-administered drug guidance and does not address home infusion billing directly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual lists pharmacy-benefit operational requirements such as NCPDP billing, direct delivery, Medicaid-enrolled dispensing, and counseling, but the policy does not [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The provider manual chunk provided does not identify a single biller for every home infusion situation beyond requiring the drugs and supplies to be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit.

NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_55.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_55.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs and supplies. They must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the pharmacy is responsible for billing the correct pathway when pharmacy coverage applies.
answers.policy_definition:
The policy is broader and does not address home infusion directly. It says certain practitioner-administered drugs are covered when they meet FDA or medically accepted indication requirements and the applicable billing guidance is met.

The policy is less specific and only gives general practitioner-administered drug coverage rules. It says reimbursement is available when the clinical criteria in the Medicaid practitioner-administered drug policies and billing guidance are met.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion drugs, supplies, and services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.

Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit if they meet pharmacy billing criteria. The drug must be billable in NCPDP format, delivered to the place of administration, excluded from a facility rate, and dispensed by a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy.
similarities: Both sources tie payment to Medicaid billing rules rather than a simple automatic payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy update is about practitioner-administered drugs and is therefore only a partial match [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives detailed pharmacy-benefit billing conditions, while the policy update only points to broader clinical coverage and billing guidance for administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
comparison: Both sources tie payment to Medicaid billing rules rather than a simple automatic payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment, while the policy update is about practitioner-administered drugs and is therefore only a partial match [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives detailed pharmacy-benefit billing conditions, while the policy update only points to broader clinical coverage and billing guidance for administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The policy source is only a partial match because it addresses practitioner-administered drugs in general, not home infusion specifically.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

test_id_56.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone reimbursement is not directly addressed in the retrieved policy chunk set. The newest provider manual says that if naloxone is dispensed as a drug, Medicaid reimburses the medication when billed by NDC, and any administration claim must be billed separately.
answers.policy_definition: The policy chunk does not address naloxone billing or pharmacy reimbursement directly. It only says that MMCPs will reimburse an initial authorization of 96 units, or 24 hours of service, for 60 days after notification from the HCBS provider. That is a broader authorization rule and not a naloxone-specific payment rule.
answers.provider_manual_definition: For a drug claim, the pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication when it is billed via National Drug Code using the reimbursement methodology. If administration is also billed, the claims must be submitted separately. The administration claim uses procedure code 96372 and is reimbursed the current administration fee.
similarities: Both sources discuss Medicaid reimbursement mechanics rather than clinical guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct billing rule for a dispensed drug and separate administration billing, while the policy chunk only addresses an authorization-based reimbursement concept for HCBS services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
comparison: Both sources discuss Medicaid reimbursement mechanics rather than clinical guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].

The provider manual gives a direct billing rule for a dispensed drug and separate administration billing, while the policy chunk only addresses an authorization-based reimbursement concept for HCBS services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
answers.caveats: No retrieved policy chunk provides a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule, so the policy side is only a partial match.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

submitting a void to the previously paid claim.

test_id_56.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_56.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_56.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

test_id_57.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone is reimbursed based on how it is billed. The drug itself is reimbursed when submitted with the ordering prescriber’s NPI and billed through NDC reimbursement methodology, while administration is billed separately.
answers.policy_definition: The policy chunk addresses reimbursement in a different context. It says MMCPs reimburse initial authorization of HCBS service units for 60 days after notification from the HCBS provider. It does not speak to naloxone or pharmacy drug billing.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The naloxone drug claim must use the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The medication is reimbursed when billed via National Drug Code using reimbursement methodology. If administration is billed, it must be submitted separately.
similarities: Both sources describe a reimbursement rule that depends on how a claim is authorized or submitted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific naloxone billing method, including the ordering prescriber’s NPI and NDC reimbursement, while the policy chunk is broader and applies to HCBS service units instead of naloxone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
comparison: Both sources describe a reimbursement rule that depends on how a claim is authorized or submitted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].

The provider manual gives a specific naloxone billing method, including the ordering prescriber’s NPI and NDC reimbursement, while the policy chunk is broader and applies to HCBS service units instead of naloxone [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy evidence does not directly address naloxone reimbursement, so the policy side is only a partial reimbursement comparison.

policy.text:
Reimbursement for the cost of the vaccine for members 19 years of age and older will be
made at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:

for initial authorization of 96 units or a total
of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days
from the time that the MMCP receives
notification from the HCBS provider.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
